content.text: The major incident executive review is a formal, time-bounded meeting held within 72 hours of resolving a major incident — typically SEV-1, multi-hour, and customer-visible — attended by the Incident Commander, team leads, and engineering and product executives. Its purpose is to give organizational leadership the information they need to make resource and commitment decisions, not to replicate or replace the technical blameless postmortem that the engineering team conducts separately.

The meeting is structured around a two-page executive brief prepared by the IC before the review. That brief translates the technical postmortem into business language: customer impact in concrete numbers, SLA breach hours, customer commitments made during the incident, systemic risk assessment, and specific resource or investment asks that require executive approval. The technical root cause appears only as a one-sentence summary — the detail lives in the postmortem, not the exec review.

attrs.alt: Major incident executive review: preparation and meeting flow from technical postmortem to exec outputs

content.text: Executives attending a major incident debrief have fundamentally different information needs than engineers attending a blameless postmortem. Engineers need to understand the full technical causal chain so they can build better detections and defenses. Executives need to understand customer impact, whether commitments made during the incident are being honored, what the organization's legal and regulatory exposure is, and whether the underlying systemic risk warrants investment that requires their approval.

When these two audiences are merged into a single meeting, the outcome is consistently poor. Engineers hold back technically precise discussion because it confuses executives; executives skip the meeting because the technical depth is impenetrable. The result is that neither group gets what they need, and decisions that require executive authority — headcount additions, product delays, customer credit programs — remain unmade for weeks after the incident closes.
resource and commitment decisions that normally take weeks surface at the 72-hour mark while organizational urgency is still high.
commitments made to customers during the incident are explicitly reviewed and either confirmed or corrected before CS teams follow up.
executives gain awareness of technical debt and monitoring gaps that create business risk, enabling prioritization decisions that pure engineering forums cannot make.
incidents requiring customer or regulatory notification are reviewed with Legal present, reducing the risk of notification timing errors.
executive participation signals to the engineering team that major incidents matter at the highest level, reinforcing the culture of incident seriousness.

The exec review creates preparation overhead on the IC, who typically needs two to four hours after incident resolution to write the executive brief while also coordinating postmortem scheduling, handling customer communication follow-ups, and recovering from what was likely a multi-hour high-stress response. Organizations must explicitly protect IC time for brief preparation — if the IC is immediately pulled into the next on-call shift or a full-day sprint review, the brief will be rushed and the exec review loses much of its value.

A second trade-off is executive meeting fatigue. If every SEV-2 incident triggers an exec review, leadership disengages. The exec review should be reserved for incidents that are genuinely major: multi-hour SEV-1s, incidents causing SLA breaches, incidents resulting in customer data exposure, or incidents where customer commitments were made that require follow-through. Well-functioning teams run one to three exec reviews per quarter, not one per week.

The process begins at incident resolution. The IC is responsible for scheduling the exec review within 72 hours and starting the executive brief within 24 hours. The brief is the primary preparation artifact — a two-page maximum document that summarizes impact (customers affected, downtime duration, SLA breach hours, estimated revenue impact), provides a business-narrative timeline (no technical jargon), summarizes the root cause in one sentence, lists customer commitments made during the incident, identifies systemic risk, and states any resource or investment asks explicitly.

The meeting itself runs 45 to 60 minutes. The IC opens with the impact summary (10 minutes). The L3 lead adds the systemic risk context (10 minutes). Customer Success reviews the commitments and current customer communication status (10 minutes). Legal flags any notification obligations (5 minutes). The remaining time is for executive questions and resource ask approvals. Decisions are captured in writing before the meeting ends. The meeting does not re-litigate technical root cause — that belongs in the postmortem.

The exec review sits as a parallel track to the blameless postmortem. Both processes start from the same incident timeline source — the incident Slack channel log, the monitoring event log, and the IC's incident notes. The postmortem extracts the full technical causal chain from this source. The executive brief extracts the business impact and decision-relevant facts from the same source.

The two documents are maintained separately and have different distribution lists. The postmortem is distributed to the full engineering team and is public within the organization. The executive brief is distributed to executive attendees and the CS lead, and may have restricted distribution if it contains customer-specific data or legal hold material. Action items from both documents are tracked in the same system — typically Jira or Linear — with clear tagging to distinguish postmortem action items from exec-approved items.

Trigger an exec review for any incident that meets one or more of the following criteria: duration exceeded two hours at SEV-1; more than 1% of active users were affected; a public SLA was breached; customer-specific commitments (credits, dedicated support, remediation timelines) were made during the incident; customer data was exposed or potentially exposed; the incident involved a safety boundary violation by an agentic AI system; or the incident is likely to trigger regulatory notification requirements.

Do not trigger an exec review for incidents that were contained quickly, had no customer-visible impact, required no customer commitments, and present no systemic risk that requires executive resource allocation. Keeping the bar high preserves the significance of the review and maintains executive engagement over time.
the IC sends a calendar invite to the exec review distribution list, blocking a 60-minute slot within 72 hours. Attaches a stub brief with placeholders.
IC completes the executive brief. CS lead reviews the commitments section. Legal reviews if data exposure or notification is implicated.
brief is shared with all attendees for pre-reading. Each section owner reviews their material and prepares a 5-minute verbal summary.
a designated scribe (not the IC) records decisions and action items in real time. Attendees reference the written brief rather than relying on verbal summary alone.
scribe sends a decision summary to all attendees. Action items are entered into the issue tracker. The final executive brief is filed alongside the postmortem.

# Executive Brief: [Incident ID] — [One-line description]

**Date of incident:** YYYY-MM-DD  
**Duration:** X hours Y minutes (SEV-1: T+0m to T+Xm)  
**Prepared by:** [IC Name]  
**Review meeting:** [Date, Time, Room/Link]

---

## Impact Summary

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Customers affected | [N unique customers / % of MAU] |
| Downtime (user-visible) | [X hours Y minutes] |
| SLA breach | [Yes — X hours over SLO / No] |
| Estimated revenue impact | [$X — methodology: [brief note]] |
| Regions affected | [list] |

---

## Incident Timeline (Business Narrative)

- **[Time]** First user-facing impact begins ([plain-language description])
- **[Time]** Monitoring alert fires; on-call team engaged
- **[Time]** Severity escalated to SEV-1; incident commander assigned
- **[Time]** Mitigation deployed; impact reduced
- **[Time]** Full service restoration confirmed
- **[Time]** Customer communications sent

---

## Root Cause (One Sentence)

[Non-technical one-sentence root cause. Example: "A configuration change to the model routing layer caused all agent requests to be directed to an overloaded endpoint, resulting in timeouts for all users."]

Full technical root cause: [link to postmortem]

---

## Customer Commitments Made During the Incident

| Customer | Commitment | Made by | Status |
|----------|------------|---------|--------|
| [Name] | [e.g., service credit of $X] | [CS rep] | Pending approval |
| [Name] | [e.g., dedicated postmortem call] | [IC] | Scheduled [date] |

---

## Systemic Risk Assessment

**Recurrence likelihood:** [High / Medium / Low] — [one-sentence rationale]
**Time to recurrence if no action:** [estimate]
**Similar systems at risk:** [list if applicable]

---

## Action Items and Resource Asks

| Action | Owner | Due date | Requires exec approval? |
|--------|-------|----------|-------------------------|
| [Engineering action] | [Name] | [Date] | No |
| [Headcount ask: +1 SRE] | VP Eng | [Date] | Yes — decision needed today |
| [Customer credit program] | CPO + Legal | [Date] | Yes — decision needed today |
The following Python script generates the impact summary section from structured incident data:

from datetime import datetime
from typing import Any


def format_duration(minutes: float) -> str:
    h = int(minutes // 60)
    m = int(minutes % 60)
    if h > 0:
        return f"{h}h {m}m"
    return f"{m}m"


def generate_impact_summary(
    incident: dict[str, Any],
    sla_threshold_minutes: float = 60.0,
    revenue_per_user_per_hour: float = 0.02,
) -> str:
    """Generate the impact summary section of the executive brief."""
    start = datetime.fromisoformat(incident["anomaly_start"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
    end = datetime.fromisoformat(incident["resolved"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
    duration_min = (end - start).total_seconds() / 60

    sla_breach_min = max(0.0, duration_min - sla_threshold_minutes)
    revenue_impact = (
        incident["customers_affected"] * (duration_min / 60) * revenue_per_user_per_hour
    )

    lines = [
        "## Impact Summary\n",
        f"| Metric | Value |",
        "|--------|-------|" ,
        f"| Customers affected | {incident['customers_affected']:,} "
        f"({incident.get('pct_mau', 'N/A')}% of MAU) |",
        f"| Duration (user-visible) | {format_duration(duration_min)} |",
        f"| SLA breach | "
        + (f"Yes — {format_duration(sla_breach_min)} over SLO" if sla_breach_min > 0 else "No")
        + " |",
        f"| Estimated revenue impact | ${revenue_impact:,.0f} |",
        f"| Regions affected | {', '.join(incident.get('regions', ['unknown']))} |",
    ]
    return "\n".join(lines)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    incident = {
        "anomaly_start": "2024-09-14T02:18:00Z",
        "resolved": "2024-09-14T06:07:00Z",
        "customers_affected": 8420,
        "pct_mau": 3.2,
        "regions": ["us-east-1", "eu-west-1"],
    }
    print(generate_impact_summary(incident, sla_threshold_minutes=60))

A company operating a customer-facing agentic AI assistant for enterprise clients experienced a 4.5-hour SEV-1 in which a prompt injection attack caused the agent to exfiltrate internal metadata from tool responses into user-visible outputs for 14 enterprise accounts. The technical postmortem identified the specific prompt pattern, the missing input sanitization step, and the two alerting gaps that delayed detection.

The executive brief for the review 48 hours later communicated a different set of facts: 14 enterprise accounts were exposed to a potential data leak; all 14 were notified within 6 hours; a data breach assessment was in progress with Legal; two accounts had SLA breach events totaling 6.5 hours combined; one account had already requested a credit and a dedicated postmortem call. The systemic risk section noted that the class of vulnerability — model output as a covert channel — applied to three other agent deployments currently in production. The resource asks were: Legal approved to engage external counsel for breach assessment; one security engineer from the platform team temporarily reallocated to AI safety hardening for 8 weeks.

Both resource asks were approved in the meeting. Without the exec review, the Legal escalation would likely have taken three more days to navigate and the security reallocation would have required a separate headcount discussion two weeks later.
Turning it into a technical postmortem:
the IC walks through the full causal chain in engineering language, executives disengage, the meeting runs over, and no decisions are made.
without a written brief distributed 48 hours before, executives attend cold and the first 20 minutes of a 60-minute meeting is consumed by orientation rather than decision-making.
commitments made verbally by CS reps or the IC during the incident are not reviewed or confirmed, leading to inconsistent customer follow-through.
the IC is simultaneously presenting and trying to capture decisions, resulting in incomplete action item records.
even in the executive review, naming individuals as the cause of the incident violates blameless culture and chills candid reporting of future incidents.
Keep the brief to two pages. Anything longer will not be read before the meeting. Material that does not fit belongs in the postmortem link.
Always quantify impact. "Several customers were affected" is not exec-level communication. "8,420 customers, representing 3.2% of MAU, experienced service unavailability for 4.5 hours" is.
State resource asks explicitly, early, and with a yes/no decision format. Executives will not volunteer resource allocation — the IC must ask for specific things.
Invite Legal only when the incident has data exposure, regulatory reporting, or customer contract implications. Routine reliability incidents do not need Legal present.

For agentic AI incidents, add a brief "model behavior explanation" section that explains, in plain terms, why the agent behaved as it did. Executives cannot assess AI risk without understanding what the model was trying to do.

The exec review process is lightweight by design — one structured document and one 60-minute meeting. The scalability challenge appears when an organization grows to multiple product lines and multiple teams, each of which may have its own major incidents. A single exec review process can cover multiple teams if the VP Engineering acts as a consistent attendee and the brief template is standardized across teams. When teams become fully autonomous divisions with their own executive leadership, they should run their own reviews — a central review spanning all products becomes too general to produce actionable decisions.

The executive brief is most maintainably stored in the same document system as the blameless postmortem — Confluence, Notion, or a version-controlled Markdown repository. Keeping both documents in the same system with cross-links makes it easy for engineers to verify that the exec brief's impact numbers match the postmortem timeline data.

Impact quantification scripts (like the example above) can be wired into the incident tracking system as a one-click report generator, producing a pre-filled brief stub that the IC refines rather than writes from scratch. Incident management platforms like Firehydrant provide exec summary report templates that automate portions of this. For agentic AI incidents, supplement these tools with a query against the model evaluation database to include quality metric degradation periods in the impact calculation — infrastructure downtime alone may understate the actual user-visible impact window.

The executive brief may contain customer names, account data, and revenue impact estimates that are confidential. Distribute it only to named attendees and store it with access controls equivalent to those on customer contracts. Do not share the brief with the full engineering team — they have access to the postmortem, which contains everything they need. The two documents serve different distribution scopes by design.

For agentic AI incidents involving potential data exfiltration or model safety events, the executive brief may also be subject to legal hold once external counsel is engaged. In that case, creation and distribution of the brief should go through Legal before it is shared with non-engineering attendees. Establish this workflow — and the Legal contact — before the first major incident occurs, not during it.

Think of the blameless postmortem and the executive review as two different lenses trained on the same incident. The postmortem is a microscope: it examines the technical causal chain at high resolution to find every failure mode and every missed detection. The executive review is a wide-angle lens: it steps back to see how the incident appeared to customers and the business, what organizational resources need to be realigned, and whether the incident reveals a systemic risk that the current product roadmap is not addressing.

Neither lens produces the full picture alone. The postmortem without the exec review leaves resource and commitment decisions unmade. The exec review without the postmortem produces resource decisions disconnected from technical reality. The two-track process is the complete system, and the two-page executive brief is the translation artifact that makes it work.
The executive review is a separate process from the blameless postmortem, with a different audience, different language, and different decision output.
The two-page executive brief — prepared within 24 hours, distributed 48 hours before the meeting — is the critical preparation artifact.
The meeting agenda has five parts: impact summary, systemic risk, customer commitments, Legal flags, resource ask decisions.
Reserve the exec review for truly major incidents — multi-hour SEV-1s, SLA breaches, data exposure, agentic AI safety events.
For agentic AI, add a plain-language model behavior explanation to the brief and address AI-specific regulatory notification requirements explicitly.
The executive brief has a tighter access control than the postmortem — it may contain customer names, revenue data, and legal hold material.
